agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled relative to its lifecycle states (placed, invoiced, shipped).","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing further fulfillment. Intended to be available before shipment per Product requirements."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document locking in pricing for an order. Described by Finance as irreversible once created."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this moment."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The act of dispatching goods to the customer. Product defines this as the cancellation cutoff."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — PM says this should succeed (not yet shipped), but Finance says it must fail (already invoiced at placement per Ops)."},{"scenario":"Order placed, not yet shipped, customer requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — same contradiction applies. Every placed-but-not-shipped order is also an invoiced order."},{"scenario":"Credit notes or refunds issued against an invoice","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — if Finance permits credit notes against invoices, cancellation-via-credit-note might resolve the contradiction, but no stakeholder has mentioned this mechanism."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — if cancellation is blocked by invoicing, customers cannot cancel orders at all after placement, leading to potential chargebacks, refund disputes, and customer attrition. If invoicing is made reversible, Finance loses pricing lock-in guarantees and audit trail integrity.","operational":"HIGH — warehouse and fulfillment teams cannot receive a clear signal on whether to proceed with or halt order processing. No consistent rule exists for them to follow.","user":"HIGH — customers expect to cancel orders before shipment (industry standard). If the system silently prevents all cancellations, customer satisfaction and trust will erode rapidly.","risk":"CRITICAL — implementing any interpretation without resolving the contradiction will either violate financial controls (reversing invoices) or violate customer-facing commitments (blocking all cancellations). Either path carries regulatory, legal, or reputational risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders if Finance rule is enforced literally, contradicting reasonable expectations set by the Product requirement."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If invoicing is bypassed or reversed to allow cancellation, their pricing lock-in and audit trail guarantees are broken."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Cannot define a stable fulfillment workflow without knowing whether placed-and-invoiced orders can still be cancelled."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will face escalations from customers unable to cancel, with no clear policy to communicate."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Cancellation feature cannot ship as specified without resolving the conflict."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Three authoritative stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other, creating a logical impossibility. If invoicing happens at placement (Ops) and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (Finance), then the PM's requirement that orders are cancellable before shipment can never be satisfied. No amount of analysis can resolve this without stakeholder alignment. Confidence is well below 50 due to the hard contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unambiguous — it is not an interpretation issue","The three statements, taken together, make cancellation impossible in all post-placement states","Industry norm supports the PM's intent (cancel before ship)"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept credit notes as a workaround (not mentioned)","Whether Operations could decouple invoicing from placement (e.g., invoice at ship)","Whether 'invoiced' and 'cancelled' could coexist if a reversal journal entry is created","The actual order lifecycle states in the system beyond what is described"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder statements are meant to be taken literally and are currently in force","There is no existing credit-note or invoice-voiding mechanism that stakeholders forgot to mention","'Invoicing' as used by Finance and Operations refers to the same concept"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. PM says cancel before ship. Finance says never cancel after invoice. Ops says invoice happens at placement. Therefore: every order is invoiced the moment it is placed, making every order uncancellable — directly contradicting the PM's requirement. These three rules cannot coexist.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Decouple invoicing from placement — invoice at shipment instead, preserving PM's cancellation window. Requires Operations to accept pricing risk between placement and shipment.","B: Introduce credit notes — allow cancellation of invoiced orders by issuing a credit note against the invoice rather than reversing it. Finance keeps invoice immutability; PM gets cancellation. Requires Finance to agree that credit notes satisfy their 'irreversible' constraint.","C: Introduce a pre-invoice hold state — orders are placed in a 'pending' state before invoicing, with a cancellation window. Invoicing is deferred (e.g., to pick/pack). Requires Operations to accept a delay in pricing lock-in.","D: Restrict cancellation to a narrow time window — allow cancellation only within minutes of placement, before the async invoice is generated. Requires PM to accept a very limited cancellation window."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit notes) is the most common industry resolution. It preserves invoice immutability (Finance is satisfied), allows order cancellation (PM is satisfied), and does not require changing when invoicing occurs (Ops is satisfied). However, this MUST be validated with all three stakeholders before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot define acceptance criteria — the three stakeholder requirements create a logical impossibility where no order can ever be both cancellable and compliant with all stated rules.","business_rule_broken":"PM: 'cancel before ship' is negated by Finance: 'no cancel after invoice' combined with Ops: 'invoice at placement'. The conjunction of all three rules produces an empty set of cancellable orders."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled relative to its lifecycle states (placed, invoiced, shipped).","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment."
